danger_signals.visual_form: Factories visibly grow and change appearance over time
danger_signals.meaning: Enemy infrastructure escalating threat level; player must act urgently

danger_signals.visual_form: Gun turrets, fighter jets, guided missiles, and battleship appearance on screen
danger_signals.meaning: Direct threats to player aircraft; increasing danger with each factory left intact

danger_signals.visual_form: Visible movement of supply vessels between factory locations
danger_signals.meaning: Enemy infrastructure interdependence and resource flow; factories strengthening each other

danger_signals.visual_form: New enemy weaponry appears as factories develop
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating difficulty and threat if factories left operational too long

safety_signals.visual_form: Isolated island location with resupply capability
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for player to recover and rearm between factory attacks

safety_signals.visual_form: Starting location and return point for player helicopter
safety_signals.meaning: Allied faction presence; strategic objective to defend

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Aircraft carrier serves as base and home location; represents allied military presence

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Gun turrets, fighter jets, guided missiles, battleship
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Six factories scattered across small planetoid; supply boats connecting industrial nodes; described as frequent villains in Broderbund games

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Factories visible at starting state across island landscape
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy units launch basic counterattacks; threat level manageable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Factories visibly grow and develop new structures; more enemy units on screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Escalating frequency and intensity of counterattacks from gun turrets and fighter jets

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Factories at peak development; battleship appears; maximum enemy weaponry visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Overwhelming counterattacks if player delays; factories create new technology faster than player can destroy

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player aircraft destroyed or unable to prevent factory conquest
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Bungeling Empire develops war machine to conquer planet Earth; mission failure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All six factories destroyed before escalation reaches critical point
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy infrastructure neutralized; threat eliminated before overwhelming player

environmental_storytelling.example: Supply boats visibly moving between islands demonstrate economic interdependence of enemy infrastructure; destroying one factory impacts others
Factories grow and change over time, showing industrial escalation and technological advancement; visual indicator of threat urgency
Player launches from allied aircraft carrier against enemy-occupied planetoid; implicit narrative of territorial conflict and military intervention
Gun turrets, fighter jets, missiles, and battleship represent tier progression of military capability; harder enemies appear as player delays action

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Grow and develop new defensive technology; send supply boats to other factories; launch stronger counterattacks over time
Defend factories with defensive fire against player helicopter
Pursue and engage player aircraft; escalate in number as factories develop
Launched at player aircraft; more frequent with factory advancement
Appears as final defensive asset when factories reach peak development; represents overwhelming military force
Can retreat to aircraft carrier or hidden island for resupply; must bomb factories before escalation becomes unmanageable

notes: This is a 1987 NES port of Will Wright's 1984 Commodore 64 original. The game communicates world-state primarily through visible factory development, escalating enemy presence, and visible supply infrastructure. The political situation is implicit: player represents allied force attacking enemy-held territory. The core tension is temporal—factories strengthen over time, so delay is punished through increasingly difficult combat. The microphone feature in Japanese 2-player mode (triggering fighter jet attacks) suggests political/propaganda themes but is not extensively documented in sources. Game lacks explicit faction color coding or symbolic heraldry; instead uses spatial positioning (player base vs. enemy islands) and technological progression (destructible infrastructure vs. defensive weaponry) to communicate conflict.
sources_used: tcrf - Raid on Bungeling Bay (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
